plainMeaning: Just as a meditating yogi merges into the inner sound, so should the devotee become wholly pure consciousness — and then nothing at all is hard; this is spoken from real experience.
recitation: yathā nādamayo yogī, tathā caitanya-mayo bhavet | tadā na duṣkaraṁ kiñcit, kathyate'nubhavād idam ||

wordByWord.gloss: just as, in the manner that
wordByWord.etymology: Relative correlative adverb from pronominal stem ya- + suffix -thā ('in which way'); pairs with tathā ('in that way').
wordByWord.notes: Opens a yathā…tathā simile that structures the whole first line.

wordByWord.gloss: made of / absorbed into inner sound (nāda)
wordByWord.etymology: Bahuvrīhi/tatpuruṣa nāda-maya (nāda 'subtle inner sound' + -maya 'consisting of, pervaded by'), nom. sg. masc. nādamayaḥ; final visarga → o before voiced yogī.
wordByWord.notes: nāda is a preface-glossed term; here it is the unstruck inner sound (anāhata-nāda) into which the absorbed yogi dissolves.

wordByWord.etymology: From yoga ('union, discipline'; √yuj 'to yoke') + possessive -in; nom. sg. masc. yogī.

wordByWord.gloss: so too, in the same way
wordByWord.etymology: Correlative adverb from demonstrative ta- + -thā; the answering term to yathā.
Tatpuruṣa caitanya-maya (caitanya 'consciousness, sentience'; from cetana, √cit 'to be aware' + -maya), nom. sg. masc. caitanyamayaḥ → caitanyamayo.
wordByWord.notes: The book prints the metrically contracted ચૈતન્મયો (caitanmayo); read as caitanya-mayo to balance nādamayo in the simile.
The optative gives the line its prescriptive force — an instruction, not mere description.

wordByWord.etymology: Temporal adverb from demonstrative ta- + -dā ('at that time').
Primary negative particle, Vedic and classical Sanskrit.
wordByWord.notes: Negates duṣkaraṁ across the whole clause: nothing remains difficult.

wordByWord.etymology: duṣ- ('ill, hard') + -kara ('doing, to be done'; √kṛ); nom./acc. sg. neut. duṣkaram.
Indefinite kim ('what') + enclitic -cit; here with na ('not anything').
Passive present 3rd sg. of √kath ('to tell, relate').
anubhava ('direct perception, lived experience'; anu- + √bhū) in the ablative sg. anubhavāt; initial a- elided after kathyate (avagraha ઽ).
wordByWord.notes: Sandhi: kathyate + anubhavāt → kathyate'nubhavāt; -āt + idam → -ād idam.
Refers back to the whole claim of the verse — the teaching now being stated.

literalTranslation: Just as the yogi is made of (absorbed in) the inner sound, so may he become made of pure consciousness; then nothing whatsoever is difficult — this is declared from direct experience.
idiomaticTranslation: As a yogi dissolves utterly into the inner sound, so let the devotee become nothing but pure awareness; once that happens, no task remains hard at all — and this I affirm not from hearsay but from lived realization.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the litany of dispelled fears in v.9, this verse turns inward to name the highest fruit of the practice: total absorption. The poet builds a precise simile (yathā…tathā). The first term is drawn from contemplative experience everyone in the tradition recognizes — the yogi who, listening to the unstruck inner sound (nāda), ceases to be a hearer apart from what is heard and becomes 'sound-made' (nādamayaḥ). The answering term raises this to its spiritual summit: just so, the practitioner should become 'consciousness-made' (caitanya-mayaḥ), no longer a self observing awareness but awareness itself. The optative bhavet keeps it an instruction, the goal toward which the preceding mantra-meditation (vv.3–8) was aimed. The second line states the consequence and then certifies it. 'Then nothing whatsoever is difficult' (tadā na duṣkaraṁ kiñcit): once the meditator rests as pure consciousness, the entire weight of obstacle, fear, and effort that the hymn has been working to remove simply has no purchase — there is no separate, anxious self left for whom anything could be 'hard.' The closing kathyate'nubhavād idam is the verse's signature: this is asserted anubhavāt, 'from direct experience,' not from inference or scriptural report. The poet stakes the claim on realization, lending the whole tāntric method its experiential warrant and gently inviting the reciter to verify it personally rather than merely believe it.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse marks the pivot from sound to consciousness that the Jain path ultimately demands: nāda-absorption is a skilful means, but the goal is the soul (jīva) resting as pure cetanā — upayoga, sentient awareness, which Jain metaphysics holds to be the very svabhāva (intrinsic nature) of the self. Becoming caitanya-maya is therefore not merger into an external absolute but the soul abiding in its own unobstructed knowing. The promise that 'nothing is difficult' echoes the principle that bondage and the sense of hardship are functions of identification with the non-self; when consciousness rests in itself, karmic affliction loses its grip. Crucially, the appeal to anubhava aligns with the Jain insistence that liberating knowledge is ultimately pratyakṣa (direct), not merely paroksa (mediated) — realization, not belief, is decisive.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For the reciter, this verse reframes the whole stotra: the mantras and fear-removing names are not ends but a ladder toward inward stillness. In practice, after chanting, one lets attention settle from the words into the silent awareness behind them — moving, as the verse says, from sound-absorption to consciousness-absorption. Its quiet promise is reassurance: difficulties feel insurmountable only to the agitated self; rest as awareness and the burden lightens. The closing 'from experience' is an invitation — test it, don't merely trust it.

crossReferences.text: Hatha­yoga­pradīpikā 4.79–101 (the nādānusandhāna teaching: the yogi merging into the unstruck inner sound)
Tattvārtha­sūtra 2.8 — 'upayogo lakṣaṇam', consciousness/awareness as the defining mark of the soul
crossReferences.source: Umāsvāti, trans. N. Tatia (That Which Is)

crossReferences.text: Jñānārṇava / Yogaśāstra traditions on dhyāna culminating in self-absorption

sources.title: Nāda yoga (concept of nāda; anāhata 'unstruck' inner sound and absorption)
sources.consultedFor: Meaning of nāda as the subtle inner sound and the yogic practice of merging into it, verifying nādamaya in the simile.

sources.title: Caitanya — Sanskrit dictionary definitions (consciousness, sentience)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss and derivation of caitanya ('consciousness') underlying the compound caitanya-maya.

sources.title: Learn Sanskrit online dictionary (nāda, anubhava, duṣkara head-entries)
sources.consultedFor: Verification of root forms and senses of nāda, anubhava, duṣkara, kiñcit for the word-by-word glosses.

sources.title: Haṭhayogapradīpikā, ch. 4 (nādānusandhāna — absorption in the inner sound)
sources.consultedFor: The classical yogic doctrine of the yogi becoming one with nāda, the source-image of the verse's simile.

notes: The book prints ચૈતન્મયો (caitan-mayo), a metrically contracted spelling; it is read here as caitanya-mayo to balance nādamayo, and this is flagged rather than silently normalized. Sandhi resolved in recitation: kathyate + anubhavāt → kathyate'nubhavāt (avagraha marks the elided initial a-), and anubhavāt + idam → anubhavād idam. kiñcit may appear in some printings as કિંચિત્/કિઞ્ચિત્; sense is unaffected. The verse is Sanskrit (anuṣṭubh) embedded in the Gujarati-script source. The simile is drawn from general yogic/nāda terminology shared across Indic traditions; its application to the soul-as-pure-consciousness is the Jain reading and is so marked. Cross-reference editions are representative, not necessarily the exact recensions the author used.
